item.title: Only, She didn't record "whatever was on television", she specifically recorded news broadcasts because she believed stories were at risk of having their details changed to alter the narrative and wanted to archive as much factual information as possible.
item.dc:creator: HotPinkk

item.title: Today I lost my dad
item.description:
My dad died today. He wasn’t perfect (whose dad is), but he was a kind, funny, caring soul. He was witty right up to the end, but after a week in the hospital they told us there was no options, hospice was their recommendation and after dealing with chronic pain and a failing heart he agreed. He lasted three days, only one of which was he awake. He was my best friend, and a comfort in any kind of hard circumstance because he has been through so much himself. I am in an exhausted funk right now after having stayed by his bedside at both places, and wanted to share the wonderful memories in pictures I have of the father who meant so much.
He was a busy guy, and most of my young life I remember him as someone who probably worked too much. He did work too much, but that was so all his kids could have everything we could want, even if that was at the expense of his time and health. I’d love to be able to go back in time and ask him to take it easy, enjoy your health, but of course I can’t, and it would be selfish to do so anyway. The truth is his family was everything to him, and I as I write this with tears in my eyes I know I just wish I had more time with him.
I remember him best in the later years. After all us kids had grown up and left the house. He was a hero all my life, but more so as an adult. A sage, wiseman, who was quick tongued, and dastardly charming. And even in his old age was still sharp, although the pain throughout his body definitely took its toll on his mind. It was in the last year that working the phone became more difficult and I could tell sometimes he struggled to remember even a short time ago. He never lost his mind, but as his heart slowed and slowed it sapped a bit of the indomitable spirit I had known all my life.
I’m writing this because this man meant the world to me and I want to share him with the world. He was a guide and a best friend, a teacher and a hero. I told him as much in the last week when he still was cognizant. Attached to tubes and machines I struggled to hug him, “It’s hard to get to you, dad!” To which he responded “Yeah, but I’m worth it.” Of course he was. Dad, I miss you. I don’t know what happens now, and I’m happy you aren’t suffering anymore, but what do I do now without knowing you’re there? What happens next time I’m driving home from work and I hit speed dial to your number and you don’t pick up? Who will have those same pearls of wisdom and great stories that you did? I hope and pray I see you again someday, but that might be a long time. I hope you are at peace, and I hope you knew how loved and important you were and still are. I love you dad.
item.dc:creator: ClassicCharm1986

item.title: It's been a week and I'm struggling with sleep and not wanting to go to work
item.description: Update: I witnessed the incident on the cameras at work. The place I work has over 100 buildings amd part of my job is to monitor the cameras. The person who did it was not an employee, they were there for a singular purpose. There was another person in the area just after the incident occurred who could have saved them but unfortunately they did not see the person and left. By the time someone else arrived it was too late. I'm having trouble sleeping, I'm starting to be afraid of the dark and afraid to watch the cameras anymore. Anytime I see someone in property I start to worry. Case in point: earlier this week there was a car on property that was idling and occupied for over an hour and i called the police because I was worried they had overdosed or something. My work provides free counseling and I have an appointment in a few weeks but I made the post here because the community is very supportive and I needed to talk about it. Thank you everyone for your advice!
item.dc:creator: AntiJen360

item.title: Stuff, with a sprinkle of memes
item.description:
I'm shocked...
I know some people like this.
That is rough, sis.
Shiny, and dark
Not even me
I love doing this, it brings me joy. It also helps guys refraining from mansplaining to me. Edit: For those criticizing me for this: I only do it when men try to explain something I have already shown competency in, like in the meme. I.E. sarcastically asking what sarcasm is. It seems like all of you have your own issues to work out.
I need
Miyazaki is based, as the kids say.
Two strap on memes? I'm a switch, not just a dom.
The only thing John Hinckley did wrong was not follow through.
Most guys I know.
I would die.
Google "trump rule 34" to see the how they are handling the outcome.
I love making bread.
Is me. Except I lost my little boy. I'm not ready for another cat yet.
The first pride was a riot.
Why?!
Why is this so funny?
The struggle is real

item.dc:creator: bubblegumfaerie

item.title: Medium Laborers Dump
item.description:
I have a work site that is just one large OSHA violation and due to the cities requirements, there's not much that can be done.
Everyone in construction has at least one shared experience; a very near death encounter. Some call it luck, I call it having enough rules to make sure you are still safe even if you forget one or two of them.
Speaking of near death, saw a guy get doused in gasoline and keep working....until he fell over from being too high from it.
I don't know what my blood pressure is, but I do know it's not good.
With 10-12 hour work days, this is the least accurate meme I have found. Nobody but the Super gets 12 hours of sleep.
...
......
My favorite is when they have 3 managers with 2 years experience each, all sitting around the water cooler trying to figure out how they can make me do more work so they can do less.
Remember, Ryobi is just fine for the weekend warrior, nothing wrong with it for that use. But if you are going to use it every day, get something better.
Once a guy came over with a bag full of tamales, jalapeno, chicken, and cheese. He had dozens, I've been chasing that high ever since.

item.title: Archaeologists find records of Pompeii survivors that reveal how they rebuilt their lives
item.description:
Archaeologists find records of Pompeii survivors that reveal how they rebuilt their lives By — Steven L. Tuck, The Conversation World Jun 8, 2024 2:38 PM EDT On Aug. 24, in A.D. 79, Mount Vesuvius erupted, shooting over 3 cubic miles of debris up to 20 miles (32.1 kilometers) in the air. As the ash and rock fell to Earth, it buried the ancient cities of Pompeii and Herculaneum. According to most modern accounts, the story pretty much ends there: Both cities were wiped out, their people frozen in time. It only picks up with the rediscovery of the cities and the excavations that started in earnest in the 1740s. But recent research has shifted the narrative. The story of the eruption of Mount Vesuvius is no longer one about annihilation; it also includes the stories of those who survived the eruption and went on to rebuild their lives. The search for survivors and their stories has dominated the past decade of my archaeological fieldwork, as I've tried to figure out who might have escaped the eruption. Some of my findings are featured in an episode of the new PBS documentary, "Pompeii: The New Dig." Making it out alive Pompeii and Herculaneum were two wealthy cities on the coast of Italy just south of Naples. Pompeii was a community of about 30,000 people that hosted thriving industry and active political and financial networks. Herculaneum, with a population of about 5,000, had an active fishing fleet and a number of marble workshops. Both economies supported the villas of wealthy Romans in the surrounding countryside. In popular culture, the eruption is usually depicted as an apocalyptic event with no survivors: In episodes of the TV series "Doctor Who" and "Loki," everyone in Pompeii and Herculaneum dies. But the evidence that people could have escaped was always there. The eruption itself continued for over 18 hours. The human remains found in each city account for only a fraction of their populations, and many objects you might have expected to have remained and be preserved in ash are missing: Carts and horses are gone from stables, ships missing from docks, and strongboxes cleaned out of money and jewelry. All of this suggests that many – if not most – of the people in the cities could have escaped if they fled early enough. Some archaeologists have always assumed that some people escaped. But searching for them has never been a priority. So I created a methodology to determine if survivors could be found. I took Roman names unique to Pompeii or Herculaneum – such as Numerius Popidius and Aulus Umbricius – and searched for people with those names who lived in surrounding communities in the period after the eruption. I also looked for additional evidence, such as improved infrastructure in neighboring communities to accommodate migrants. After eight years of scouring databases of tens of thousands of Roman inscriptions on places ranging from walls to tombstones, I found evidence of over 200 survivors in 12 cities. These municipalities are primarily in the general area of Pompeii. But they tended to be north of Mount Vesuvius, outside the zone of the greatest destruction. It seems as though most survivors stayed as close as they could to Pompeii. They preferred to settle with other survivors, and they relied on social and economic networks from their original cities as they resettled. Some migrants prosper Some of the families that escaped apparently went on to thrive in their new communities. The Caltilius family resettled in Ostia – what was then a major port city to the north of Pompeii, 18 miles from Rome. There, they founded a temple to the Egyptian deity Serapis. Serapis, who wore a basket of grain on his head to symbolize the bounty of the earth, was popular in harbor cities like Ostia dominated by the grain trade. Those cities also built a grand, expensive tomb complex decorated with inscriptions and large portraits of family members. Members of the Caltilius family married into another family of escapees, the Munatiuses. Together, they created a wealthy, successful extended family.
Some of the survivors resettled in Ostia, a port city north of Pompeii. A statue of Neptune is pictured in Ostia, near Rome, Italy, April 13, 2020. File photo by Alberto Lingria via Reuters The second-busiest port city in Roman Italy, Puteoli – what's known as Pozzuoli today – also welcomed survivors from Pompeii. The family of Aulus Umbricius, who was a merchant of garum, a popular fermented fish sauce, resettled there. After reviving the family garum business, Aulus and his wife named their first child born in their adopted city Puteolanus, or "the Puteolanean." Others fall on hard times Not all the survivors of the eruption were wealthy or went on to find success in their new communities. Some had already been poor to begin with. Others seemed to have lost their family fortunes, perhaps in the eruption itself. Fabia Secundina from Pompeii – apparently named for her grandfather, a wealthy wine merchant – also ended up in Puteoli. There, she married a gladiator, Aquarius the retiarius, who died at the age of 25, leaving her in dire financial straits. Three other very poor families from Pompeii – the Avianii, Atilii and Masuri families – survived and settled in a small, poorer community called Nuceria, which goes by Nocera today and is about 10 miles (16.1 kilometers) east of Pompeii. According to a tombstone that still exists, the Masuri family took in a boy named Avianius Felicio as a foster son. Notably, in the 160 years of Roman Pompeii, there was no evidence of any foster children, and extended families usually took in orphaned children. For this reason, it's likely that Felicio didn't have any surviving family members. This small example illustrates the larger pattern of the generosity of migrants – even impoverished ones – toward other survivors and their new communities. They didn't just take care of each other; they also donated to the religious and civic institutions of their new homes. For example, the Vibidia family had lived in Herculaneum. Before it was destroyed by the eruption of Vesuvius, they had given lavishly to help fund various institutions, including a new temple of Venus, the Roman goddess of love, beauty and fertility. One female family member who survived the eruption appears to have continued the family's tradition: Once settled in her new community, Beneventum, she donated a very small, poorly made altar to Venus on public land given by the local city council. How would survivors be treated today? While the survivors resettled and built lives in their new communities, government played a role as well. The emperors in Rome invested heavily in the region, rebuilding properties damaged by the eruption and building new infrastructure for displaced populations, including roads, water systems, amphitheaters and temples. This model for post-disaster recovery can be a lesson for today. The costs of funding the recovery never seems to have been debated. Survivors were not isolated into camps, nor were they forced to live indefinitely in tent cities. There's no evidence that they encountered discrimination in their new communities. Instead, all signs indicate that communities welcomed the survivors. Many of them went on to open their own businesses and hold positions in local governments. And the government responded by ensuring that the new populations and their communities had the resources and infrastructure to rebuild their lives. The Conversation This article is republished from The Conversation under a Creative Commons license. Read the original article. https://theconversation.com/records-of-pompeiis-survivors-have-been-found-and-archaeologists-are-starting-to-understand-how-they-rebuilt-their-lives-230641 Yes, his name was "Aquarius the retiarius".

item.title: Street Tacos in Southern California
item.description: Update: Wow! thank you everyone for upvoting my lunch! This old fart doesn't go out to eat much anymore, so this is a real treat to have... I am visiting my nephew and he took me out to this wonderful hole in the wall in the LA area... my goodness, this old man has been missing out!
item.dc:creator: PositiveMiketheBear

item.title: Ori the Dwarf
item.description:
This is a lore account on the friend of Bilbo Baggins, who left a memorable record in Moria.
Though Ori was one of the Dwarves who helped to restore the lost kingdom of Erebor ["the Lonely Mountain"], and reported the last despairing days of the recolonization of Moria, nearly nothing is known about him before he met Bilbo Baggins in year 2941 of the Third Age (art by Blake Henriksen).
As the average lifespan of Dwarves was ca 250 years (The History of Middle-Earth; IX; IV) it it possible that Ori was born in the 28th century, though it is more plausible that he was born in the 29th century; but not later than year 2879 when Gimli was born, since Gimli at age sixty-two was still deemed to young to join King Thorin Oakenshield's perilous quest to Erebor in 2941 (Unfinished Tales; III; III; Appendix).
Thorin's people was the Longbeards, founded by King Durin I. Ori was the cousin of Nori and Dori, and they in an undefined manner belonged to, or at least were related to, the Longbeards' royal family: "Of the other companions of Thorin Oakenshield in the journey to Erebor Ori, Nori, and Dori were also of the House of Durin, and more remote kinsmen of Thorin..." – The Return of the King; Appendix A; III (art by Matthew Stewart)
The name of Ori and the other Dwarves in The Hobbit are from dwarfs in Dvergatal in Völuspá in the Poetic Edda, concerning Norse mythology. The meaning of the name Ori in Old Norse is "Violent". The Dwarves in Tolkien's legendarium kept their true names secret and used aliases taken from a northern human tongue when interacting with outsiders; this 'inworld' northern tongue JRR translated using Old Norse.
Before King Thorin Oakenshield set out he lived in the Blue Mountains at the coast west of the Shire; thus it is plausible that Ori and his cousins lived there at the time (art by meneldil-elda). To succeed with the quest to recapture Erebor from the dragon Smaug, who occupied it since 2770, the friendly wizard named Gandalf the Grey adviced the Dwarves to bring along a Hobbit.
The hobbit selected was Bilbo Baggins and one eve, to Bilbo's great surprise, twelve Dwarves arrived at his home, one by one or in groups (The Hobbit; I, art by Angus McBride): "He had hardly turned the knob, before they were all inside, bowing and saying “at your service” one after another. Dori, Nori, Ori, Óin, and Glóin were their names; and very soon two purple hoods, a grey hood, a brown hood, and a white hood were hanging on the pegs, and off they marched with their broad hands stuck in their gold and silver belts to join the others."
After dinner the Dwarves performed a song about about their lost home; and "Dori, Nori, and Ori brought out flutes from somewhere inside their coats" (ibid, art by the Brothers Hildebrandt). In the adventures to Erebor, the demise of Smaug, battle against Goblins and restoration of Erebor, Ori however played no individual role; he has no privat dialogues nor any personal deeds. What proved most important is that while Thorin died Ori surived, and would have the gusto to risk a second quest.
After 2941 was Erebor restored, and the Longbeards were ruled by Thorin's kinsman Dáin, yet the new prosperity was not enough for the Longbeards to find peace. The Folk of Durin could not forget that a millenium had passed since they mined far below Khazad-dûm and awoke a namless dread (a Balrog, an ancient demon), that had driven them away in 1981.
"`Moria! Moria! Wonder of the Northern world! Too deep we delved there, and woke the nameless fear. Long have its vast mansions lain empty since the children of Durin fled. But now we spoke of it again with longing, and yet with dread...." – The Fellowship of the Ring; II; II One of the companions who, like Ori and Oín, had followed Thorin Oakenshield was Balin. As was later reported, Balin in 2989 led an expedition to eastern Moria (art by Henning Janssen);
"At last... Balin listened to the whispers, and resolved to go; and though Dáin did not give leave willingly, he took with him Ori and Óin and many of our folk, and they went away south... For a while we had news and it seemed good: messages reported that Moria had been entered and a great work begun there. Then there was silence, and no word has ever come from Moria since." – Ibid (art by Tulikoura)
The last contact was in 2994, and when the Fellowship of the Ring was formed in 3018 neither Gimli or his father Glóin expressed any hope that Balin's expedition was still alive. Nor did Gimli propose that his companions should go to Moria. Instead it was Gandalf who argued for it, and had hope that Balin's folk yet lived, and due to the Wizard the companions went to Moria in 3019 (art by Donato Giancola).
At the western door the Fellowship was attacked by monstrous arms protracting from a lake, yet they managed to enter. They could not determine if the arms belonged to one or several beasts, but would learn that the Dwarves thought that the arms belonged to a single creature (art by John Howe).
In eastern Moria the Fellowship found the Dwarves of Balin's expedition long dead. In the Chamber of Mazarbul was a record book. In the 2001 film adaptation it is still in the hands of a dead Dwarf, while in the source it was found upon the ground. It had been slashed and stabbed and partly burned, and it was so stained with black and other dark marks like old blood that little of it could be read, but Gandalf saw many different writers on the pages which remained (art by Angelo Montanini).
The book told how all had gone well and the Dwarves explored and settled the halls and mines until Gandalf reached the fifth and final year of 2994. The Wizard at first thought the pages too cut and stained to read (FotR; II; V onwards, art by Tenshi-Inverse): "`Wait! Here is something: a large bold hand using an Elvish script.' `That would be Ori's hand,' said Gimli, looking over the wizard's arm. `He could write well and speedily, and often used the Elvish characters.'"
"`I fear he had ill tidings to record in a fair hand,'" said Gandalf and read aloud (art by DavidGaillet): "`The first clear word is sorrow, but the rest of the line is lost, unless it ends in estre. Yes, it must be yestre followed by day being the tenth of novembre Balin lord of Moria fell in Dimrill Dale. He went alone to look in Mirror mere. an orc shot him from behind a stone."
"we slew the orc, but many more ... up from east up the Silverlode. The remainder of the page is so blurred that I can hardly make anything out, but I think I can read we have barred the gates, and then can hold them long if, and then perhaps horrible and suffer. Poor Balin! He seems to have kept the title that he took for less than five years. I wonder what happened afterwards; but there is no time to puzzle out the last few pages. Here is the last page of all.'" Gandalf paused and sighed, for it was grim reading (art by martym);
"'I fear their end was cruel. Listen! We cannot get out. We cannot get out. They have taken the Bridge and second hall. Frár and Lóni and Náli fell there. Then there are four lines smeared so that I can only read went 5 days ago. The last lines run the pool is up to the wall at Westgate. The Watcher in the Water took Óin.
We cannot get out. The end comes, and then drums, drums in the deep. I wonder what that means. The last thing written is in a trailing scrawl of elf-letters: they are coming. There is nothing more.' Gandalf paused and stood in silent thought."
A sudden dread and a horror of the chamber fell on the Fellowship. Gandalf looked around (art by Tulikoura): "`They seem to have made a last stand by both doors,' he said; 'but there were not many left by that time. So ended the attempt to retake Moria! It was valiant but foolish. The time is not come yet. Now, I fear, we must say farewell to Balin son of Fundin. Here he must lie in the halls of his fathers.'"
A post on the Watcher in the Water https://imgur.com/gallery/oTXA6hW For further Tolkien lore posts, here are my indexes: Character bios https://imgur.com/gallery/axV2mPF Folks, events, items, etc https://imgur.com/gallery/AQK9pJL
